meta.purpose: Define reasoning chains behind silver age service principles — why common approaches fail and correct approaches work.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why a principle exists or seeks deeper rationale.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why solving psychological entry cost matters more than improving activity content.
logic: A senior evaluating whether to attend an activity makes the decision before experiencing the content.
The decision is based on projected experience: 'What will it feel like to walk into that room?'
If the projected experience is threatening (fear of not fitting in, technology anxiety, burden concern), the content quality is irrelevant — it is never experienced.
No amount of content improvement can overcome an entry cost that prevents entry in the first place.
Therefore, every resource invested in reducing psychological entry cost has higher ROI than the same resource invested in content improvement.
reasoning_chains.so_what: For every new program or activity, design the entry experience before designing the content experience. The first 30 seconds determine whether the next 60 minutes will happen.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why preserving autonomy is the non-negotiable foundation of senior services.
logic: Aging is experienced primarily as loss of control — physical, social, financial, decisional.
Every well-intentioned intervention that makes a decision for the senior confirms the narrative of decline: 'Even strangers think I can't manage.'
Once the autonomy narrative is triggered, the senior resists all help — including help they genuinely need — because accepting it costs their self-image.
Services that consistently preserve autonomy build trust. Services that bypass autonomy trigger permanent rejection.
Therefore, autonomy-supporting design is not a feature preference. It is the price of admission to the relationship.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before any intervention, ask: 'Am I doing this with them or to them?' If the answer is 'to them,' stop and redesign.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why connecting services to personal identity works better than age-based marketing.
logic: No one self-identifies primarily by age. A 75-year-old thinks of themselves as a retired teacher, a grandmother, a gardener, a traveler — not as '75.'
Age-based marketing says 'this is for old people.' Identity-based outreach says 'this is for gardeners who want to learn more.'
The senior who rejects 'senior activities' may eagerly join the same activity when it is framed by interest rather than age.
Identity bridging also provides social anchoring — the senior enters a group of people who share an interest, not just a demographic.
Therefore, always describe programs by the identity they serve, not the age they serve.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Replace 'Senior Art Class' with 'Watercolor Workshop.' Replace 'Elderly Walking Group' with 'Morning Walkers.' The program is the same. The psychological entry cost is transformed.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why understanding why people leave teaches more than understanding why they joined.
logic: People join programs because of hope and interest. People leave because of specific friction points.
Exit data reveals the actual barriers — the things that broke the promise of the entry experience.
Most programs do not collect exit data, so they keep fixing the wrong things (content, marketing, pricing) while the real barrier (psychological entry cost, autonomy threat, burden concern) persists.
One exit interview that surfaces an invisible barrier is worth 100 satisfaction surveys from current participants.
Therefore, every program should have a structured exit process that asks: 'What made it hard to keep coming?'
reasoning_chains.so_what: When someone stops attending, a personal, burden-aware follow-up is not optional — it is the most valuable data the program can collect.
